content.assessment: This proposal targets the capital-intensive competition mechanism that channels advantaged students into selective tracks (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02, 03) by redesigning admission instruments rather than the track structure itself. From an equity lens this is a second-order intervention: it addresses selection-within-the-funnel but leaves the funnel's entry points, geographic distribution, and non-applicant exclusion largely intact.
opportunities: Could be combined with outreach to non-applicant pools and geographic access measures to close the participation gap identified in F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03 and 08
strengths: Directly targets the documented SES-skewed access mechanism at the point of admission (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Recognizes that entry is a capital-intensive competition requiring pre-testing investment, and proposes to weaken that channel (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
threats: Selection may simply relocate rather than shrink: even with coaching-resistant exams, remaining selective-track structure still relocates advantage from those who fail to gain entry to those who succeed under new rules (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)

Advantaged families' documented capacity to redirect resources (coaching investment) suggests displacement of advantage into new exam formats rather than elimination, consistent with the general pattern that selection relocates rather than concentrates advantage (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
weaknesses: Does not address geographic clustering of selective schools in urban areas, which independently structures access alongside SES (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)

Elementary schools serving disadvantaged populations often do not facilitate participation at all, so an admissions-mechanism fix does not reach students who never enter the competition (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
Leaves within-track segregation dynamics (Roma concentration in weaker tracks) entirely unaddressed (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
